era_context: Early Bronze Age III, continuation of the Akkadian Empire's dominance in Mesopotamia and the Levant. Egypt remains in Dynasty VI of the Old Kingdom. The Indus Valley Civilization is at its peak. This year falls within a period of relative stability before the climate and political crises of the late 23rd century BCE.

events.title: Akkadian administrative consolidation and trade expansion
events.description: The Akkadian Empire under Naram-Sin continues to expand trade networks and consolidate administrative control. Akkadian merchants and officials operate throughout Mesopotamia, the Levant, and into Anatolia. Standardized weights, measures, and administrative practices are imposed across conquered territories. Akkadian becomes the lingua franca of administration and commerce across the empire. This period represents the height of Akkadian economic integration.

sources.name: Akkadian administrative tablets and merchant records

events.certainty_note: Akkadian economic expansion is well-documented through administrative records and archaeological evidence of trade goods. Precise dating within Naram-Sin's reign is uncertain.

events.title: Indus Valley Civilization seal production and standardization
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization produces thousands of seals with standardized iconography and undeciphered script. These seals are used for trade, administration, and possibly religious purposes. The standardization of seal design, weights, and measures across the civilization suggests a high degree of centralized coordination or shared cultural norms. Seals are exported to Mesopotamia and the Persian Gulf, indicating active trade networks.

sources.name: Indus seals from excavations at Mohenjo-daro, Harappa, and other sites

events.certainty_note: Indus seals are abundant and well-documented archaeologically. The mature phase dating is secure; this year falls within the accepted period.

events.title: Egyptian Old Kingdom pyramid and temple construction
events.description: Pyramid construction and temple building continue in Egypt under Dynasty VI, though at reduced scale and with less monumental ambition than earlier dynasties. Tombs of provincial governors show increasing wealth and autonomy, reflecting the gradual shift of power away from the central pharaonic authority. Religious texts and administrative records continue to be inscribed in tombs and temples.

sources.name: Pyramid inscriptions and tomb reliefs from Dynasty VI
Archaeological evidence from Old Kingdom sites

events.certainty_note: Egyptian chronology for this period is uncertain by ±50–100 years depending on the chronological system used.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the broader narrative of Akkadian stability in this period is complicated by evidence of climate stress (aridification) beginning in the late 23rd century BCE, which may have already been affecting agricultural productivity by this time.
historiographic_note: Sources for this year are similar to -2374: inscriptions, administrative records, and archaeology. Absolute chronology remains uncertain. The historical record is dominated by elite perspectives from Mesopotamia and Egypt; the Indus Valley Civilization's internal political and social structures remain largely unknown due to the undeciphered script.

graph_edges.note: Akkadian economic expansion and centralization contributed to the empire's vulnerability to external shocks.
